item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It speaks for this article that I was intrigued enough by it when I was first ploughing through the SCP wiki in … 2013 or 14 to still remember it now. However I had trouble finding it again; I was first searching for the tag "Foundation-made". I think it should have this tag.

+ show block
– hide block

I found it by searching for the tag "sphere".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is now six years since original posting, and I think this is still the most technical and dense skip on the list by a wide margin.

And I am fascinated that a piece that sparked so much hardcore distaste currently sits at +222.
That says something…but I'm not sure what.

Maybe that good writing evokes strong reactions, and not always positive ones?
Maybe that the wiki-membership demographics leans more towards STEM majors than English majors?
Maybe just that the best way to get attention is to start a debate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for this amazing SCP that took my brain for a science ride. My favorite skip so far, full of the allure of an anomaly that requires SCP level procedures to contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds like it has a continuous explosion in the middle or at least the energy of one. So maybe an SCP that requires alot of energy to damage (682 everyone is thinking of you) or 2419-A

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would contain something impossible to contain with concrete walls and bulletproof glass.
maybe scp-106?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I want to know is what they would ever need this to contain. I know this was just an experiment, but how dangerous of a scip would this crazy rotating thing contain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not an infinite generator, it's a potentially infinite (but inefficient) energy storage device that has no safe way to bleed off the energy, only exploding, and will explode if it isn't supplied with increasing power over time.

Speaking of, I also like the fact that if necessary, this device isn't unstoppable and won't necessarily destroy the world. If things really get out of hand, the Foundation can probably figure out a way to move it to a remote area for safe detonation, up to and including space or another dimension.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isent it an infinite generator, why not use it to power stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ECRG proposal 6987, "A 1/10th Scale Research Model of ECU-6852" is attached.

Pffffffffft.

Okay, maybe I've come around on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same - love me some good ULT physics :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I must have been more dumb three years ago, because I reread the whole thing and if it doesn't make perfect sense, it at least makes enough sense to follow. And it's neato.

item.title: I have a hypothesis
item.content:encoded:
I think this is magic. SCP-1718 is actually a Ritual that something put within a Foundation researcher's mind in order to use it and contain something.

From Djoric's SCP-2845

Rituals do not work because of some underlying laws, such as those that science operates on. Rituals work because they are rituals. They work because an arbitrary set of criteria has been met with exacting care. Belief that meeting these arbitrary criteria achieves a certain end assigns power to the ritual. The actions that were once meaningless now have been assigned Meaning through their repetition and application.

SCP-1718 fills this up perfectly. But rather than infant sacrifices and misterious words, the ritual had been built with confusing physics. The Foundation's Belief gave power to the machine, and it worked.

Now, we have 2 scenarios here; either the Foundation researcher somehow designed a magical ritual by accident, or something else put the idea and some kind of bluprints in his mind, in order to use it to contain something pretty dangerous. The article leads me to believe in the latter. Some kind of intelligence manipulated them to create "the ultimate cage" to contain something far more dangerous.

In the end, the container reached its real porpuse. Now the FOundation only need to keep it working. Hell, I don't think it even needs energy to maintain itself, but it's part of the ritual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is my favorite skip I have read so far. Although one tiny nitpick: the inner gyroscope was mounted in a spherical bearing and free to rotate along any axis, so coriolis effects would not cause it to accelerate. A plausible explanation for this is that the mechanical forces due to gravity causes the inner sphere to become slightly oblate in the direction of earth's gravity, either allowing tidal interactions with the moon or creating a small asymmetric friction allowing coriolis forces to accelerate it, but you don't mention this or any other explanation for it in the article. Otherwise this is just perfect. +10 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like it, I really do. But as in-depth it is, I want to know more. I want to know what it is you had in mind that is inside of this thing as you were writing it. Why it's happened. There's a story in here that isn't being told, yet the stage is so well set for it.

item.title: I get the feeling some retro-causality might be involved.
item.content:encoded:
This is mostly headcanon without much to back it up; but I got the feeling something in the future made them create this contraption.

Like perhaps some dangerous skip that interacts backwards with time was gonna/had collide(d) with Earth and cause(d) immense destruction, serious containment breaches etc, possibly leading to the end of the world with just enough time for the foundation to realize what was happening and take some desperate measures that led to the events described in the article. The changes to the past were done with extreme precision as to make the retrocausality shockwaves reach ground-zero at the precise moment of impact, with the device in place containing the backwards-time skip towards it's own past. Then the increasing energy requirements would then be explained as the interactions of the skip with regular forward-time materials producing a feedback loop where instead of dissipating energy the interactions creates it. And all the deaths and stuff were so as to remove any remaining traces of the temporal incursion (can't let people think they could go back in time and prevent the project from being done).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The technobabble had me a little put-off (I used to love science; now I struggle to enjoy anything) and there were some chunks I didn't understand, but I upvoted anyway. That said, I'm pretty sure I've only ever downvoted like five things on this entire site, so take it for what it's worth. It is at +99 now though.
Also yeah the IMPLICATIONS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was super engaging; an excellent read from start to finish. I'm disappointed that the prototype wasn't feasible, and even more disappointed that such a large chunk of readers feel the need to dismiss it when their grasp of english and/or science is just seemingly too poor to properly appreciate it! I logged in on my phone just to up vote this. Really nicely done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have enough science background from things like Wikipedia surfing to follow the technobabble well enough to understand roughly what each bit is meant to do, even if I don't follow how it does it.

I actually love this BECAUSE of the super-dense technobabble; I'm part of the target audience. +1, but this is more like 343 in terms of being polarizing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The entire thing is far too obtuse and needlessly detailed. You really, really have to LOVE science to stay with the author.

Well, that's because such people are the target audience of this one, as I have mentioned years ago in this thread.

Not everything is intended for everyone and if you always go for mass appeal, you'll never create anything that pleases anyone but the lowest common denominator of your audience. After all, the article is successful - it sits at +85 at the moment.

EDIT: To clarify - feel free to dislike it and downvote, but like, I don't think the sort of condescension as if the author has dun goof'd, is the least merited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of entries have a problem where the tone isn't scientific enough. This is the exact opposite. It is far too scientific. Having read the entire entry, I still have no clue what the SCP is, what its properties are and I'm not even sure what it looks like.

The entire thing is far too obtuse and needlessly detailed. You really, really have to LOVE science to stay with the author.

I mean, when you have a paragraph opening with:

The hyperreflective surface of the innermost shell suppresses incident radiation across a wide frequency range via █████████ ███████ interference.

I just wonder what the fuck I'm reading and who the Hell would actually want to read the rest? I really had to force myself to read through the whole thing because I wanted to give it the beenefit of the doubt and was hoping that everything would make sense at the end. Reading through entries should not feel like a chore.

I honestly, no joke, started thinking that maybe the twist would be that the SCP's effect is to make people replace what they'd normally write with techno-babble.

It kind of reminds me of the movie "Primer": there might be an interesting story in there and I'm sure the science is as accurate as possible but the overly academic language will alienate anyone who isn't fully immersed in this discipline.

I'll leave you with one of the most important rules of storytelling: Keep It Simple, Stupid!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It applies at all scales, but smaller systems tend to be affected more strongly by other forces. If all other forces were removed or canceled out (as the containment unit seems designed to do), the Coriolis effect could cause a noticeable change over enough time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't the Coriolis effect only apply to large systems like hurricanes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am another graphic design/english lit nerd. YET I really enjoyed this one. I had to tab over to wikipedia on a number of occasions, but much like early Niven and Asimov, the science sucked me in.

And there are clues; the power usage listed at the beginning (constructing dedicated powerplant) is considerably larger than those mentioned in the Spec, startup or runtime. Not until the very end is it noted that the power needs started climbing. A little corner of my mind went "why do we need so much power?" the first time through. After rounding the bend, that nagging curiosity gave me SO much satisfaction.

I do have one major question. It is not uncommon for authors to release spoilers for key redactions in comments. WHICH SCP was this supposedly designed for?? Overly curious reader wants to know; why did we need to build this for something that is already contained?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
pretty much the gold standard for hard-science skips. A lot of the effort I've pit into mine are due to the inspiration provided by this one
+10

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the irony of the containment device containing itself.

As thick as this article was, I do love it and will upvote because I enjoyed it. Whether or not someone else understands it is their problem.

This is now one of my favorite articles on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They explain it thoroughly in the older comments, but I'll give a whack at it, even though physics isn't my thing1.

cough

The Foundation tried to make a super containment unit, and they successfully got it running, then the lead scientist got amnesticized due to an unrelated incident, came back to the project, and said that the science used to develop it doesn't even make sense. So, they shut it down, but then they discovered that there's somehow a powerful explosive force inside the core, so they can't shut it down because whatever that is would escape. The force is continuously getting stronger, so they have to keep the device running to contain it. So the containment unit itself is the scip, more or less.

Footnotes
1. Hilarious, because, in-universe, Dr. Anborough is a nuclear physicist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really have no idea what's going on here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with Photosynthetic, but as I liked it, I'm upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally, I love this thing. The "technobabble" is engaging, the premise is full of fridge horror, and it was just plain fun to read. Delightful, and man, do I ever wish I could upvote.

That said, my enjoyment of this article does not preclude its being quite opaque to a large proportion of the SCP project's readership. (I am a gigantic science nerd. I also suspect, upon reflection, that part of my enjoyment stems from pride at having wrapped my head around something this technical and this far outside my field of study.) That opacity is a flaw — one serious enough to force my non-vote.

I really want to upvote this, though. There has got to be a way to preserve the engineering and physics detail while making the article more transparent. I might recommend looking to SCP-1512 for inspiration; despite dealing with a subject at least as esoteric as this one, it's at +75 with only a handful of downvotes, which suggests that it's pretty broadly accessible. I am also willing to help personally; wiki-PM me, and although I'm going to be godawful busy for the next ten days or so, I'll find what time I can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. I suppose, as an engineer, I'm within the target audience for the technobabble. And it raises that fun bit of tension of the "Push to test. Release to detonate." situation. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'll gain a lot more upvotes if you use my suggested description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know that I can go along with that line of thought.
After all, if someone doesn't enjoy an article, for whatever reason, by definition it was not "a good one" for that particular reader. Tastes differ, and there is no way to say that a piece is objectively "good" or "bad". It is only in the subjective experience of individuals that such terms have any meaning at all.

But anyway, talk about getting off topic! This article appeals to the techies and does not appeal to other tastes, and that's cool. To each their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe the tech geeks like this one for the sciency bits, but as a marketing/graphic design nerd, I see nothing to grab my attention. It's basically the slowly exploding bomb from Invader Zim played seriously, if I understand what's going on here correctly. "Ohgodohgod we fucked up" should probably be played up more, and the sciency bits backed up, if you want it to be a bit more widely accessible. Also, what the hell happened to the original thing this was supposed to hold?

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That point hadn't actually come to mind, and it really is a good point. However, I dont personally believe in downvoting a good article because it doesn't tailor to my specific wants/likes. But that's a completely personal preference and I don't expect everyone to conform to my ways, it just irks me I suppose, when somebody downvotes an article (A good one, not specifically this one either, at that) simply because they dont understand the terminology or whatever being used. No offense intended to those who downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bast, not again.
Eh. Let's put it down to you simply not being the target audience.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What wishun said.

As one of those English-major types, I say this: When the science chokes out the fiction part of science fiction, you can guarantee that I'm going to nope the hell out of there. I don't want a nine-page dissertation on exactly how your blasted spaceship works, I want the people on said spaceship to be interesting characters and I want them to do interesting things. The ship could be powered by a wizard in the engine room flapping his arms like an idiot and I am fine with that if it's telling a good story.

Yes, this is "good" on a technical level, but as far as well-written, entertaining fiction goes? By itself, when you strip away the technical specifications? It's absolutely horrendous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You have to consider differences in temprament and inclination.
For all our emphasis on (pseudo)scientific tone and presentation, this is still primarily a creative writing forum.

Some of us, myself included, like tech and science and engineering for their own sake. Since we spend so much time delving into them recreationally, we have a base-line education in the terminology and concepts. When we read this, it not only plays to our interests, it speaks a language we grok.

But a certain portion of the membership are english-major types. They are here primarily for the reading and the writing, and have no great interest in geeking out over the details of theoretical physics or cutting edge engineering. Not only do those tech specs fail to interest them, it's all terms and concepts that they may be seeing for the first time.

The (generally) peaceful coexistence of the two sects is one of the things that makes this wiki so great. The left-brains keep the writing interesting, and the right-brains keep it realistic (for signifigantly loose definitions of the word).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it. After reading through every single comment- Which took about as long as the article itself, har har har - I'm slightly perplexed as to how most of you (Which from my understanding are mostly young to middle-aged adults, some, if not most of whom, are or have attended some sort of college or university) are having trouble deciphering the "technobabble" as you put it, when a teenager who has a 10th grade Physical Science education and watches the Science Channel has little to no problem whatsoever understanding the article.

In relation to the article itself, I loved reading through all of the little and scientific details that, for me, really help make the article as good as it is.

Yes, the format is wonky, and yes the actual revelation that this extremely fast spinning indestructible ball is the skip is a little difficult to understand if you don't pay attention, but if you read through it and actually give half a hoot about what you're reading instead of going "ermahgerd wall o text noty downvote becuz 2 hard to read" it really is a well built and extremely interesting article, I mean come on. Who can't smell the deception (coughcough mindcontrol coughcough) when the head researcher got amnesiac'd and suddenly realized "Hey. Why in the actual F@#$ is this thing working, and why (how?) the F*!@ did we build it in the first place?" It's just too perfect for me.

Also, take away all of the big words and "confusing" science, and suddenly you have an article just like anything else you might find on this site. Something, that if not dealt with correctly (In this case, feed it its ever-growing power hunger) can and will be a threat to humainty (In this case, a potentially multi-megaton nuclear warhead).

Apologies for the wall of text, I just felt as though I needed to get my point sufficiently across.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably all the grad school doing that, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this thing. I didn't find the text particularly hard to penetrate; good fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first, I hated this for the same reason as most. It is completely impenetrable to read. However, after reading the discussion and re-reading the article, I found liked it for one main reason; It represents the biggest flaws of the SCP Foundation.

Their hubris, cold 'science for the sake of science' attitude, and complete disregard for scientific safety and propriety. They decided to make the ultimate containment procedure, specifically for no reason, and overreached. This is the epitome of my pragmatic view of the Foundation.

A hard earned +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, basically, the glorious and (as far as I can tell, relatively high-versimilitude) design is bollocks in-universe, and its creator has lost five years of life due to amnestics, essentially reverting to an entirely different mindstate?

Also, since inception, the device was plagued with Event-Horizonish incidents that oh-so-unluckily took the life of another head designers of the machine ?

I say it won't be a stretch to hypothesize that fine gentlemen were… influenced… by something that has elected to get rid of witnesses when the device was finally finished. That's some very cool fridge horror there.

The only complaint I have is that the device's effect itself is kind of meh - it is at this point essentially a, well, large bomb that has to be cooled in order not to blow up.

Which doesn't do the almost lovecraftian premise of "something strange was going on with the minds of people who designed it, but one is dead and the other lost a solid chunk of memory, so we can't figure out what inspired this mess in the first place" much justice.
Given the eerie build-up I'd preferred to see something weirder as "effect". Something along the lines of SCP-1782's shitfuckery or the Reagan Tape SCP. Not just a threat of a huge explosion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not slogging my way though a ridiculous amount of technical jargon I don't understand for what is basically a containment unit gone wrong.
In the real world, a document formatted like this would possibly be necessary in certain fields and organizations.
This is not the real world, and not everyone here has the knowledge or interest that this requires.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, I missed a great arguement! :( Shades of Samothrace.

I can't help but wondering if the stark division of opinion is related to science/engineering background. For example, I make my living building overly-complicated nested prototypes of no reasonably discernable purpose (mechanical effects for movies), and I loved every line of the tech-specs. But I can see how it would be an unenjoyable chore of a read if physics/math/engineering just aren't where ones interests and talents lay.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit more jargon than I'm used to, nonetheless, I was able to understand it and it's one of the best concepts I've seen on the site so far. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… get this. Just about.

My brain hurts. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now it's much too concise.

"SCP-1718 is an energetic anomaly that arose during a project managed by the ECRG5 to explore the limits of containment science."

can be read as

"It's an anomaly that happened while we were doing research. Energy is involved."

which tells us (either us-the-readers, or us-the-Foundation-personnel) just about nothing. And when you withhold information from Foundation personnel, people could die.

I strongly recommend you use the version I suggested.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seeing VAE's post, I at least see what this is trying to do. But I still have to agree with others. The technobabble is impenetrable, there's no description of what this thing is, i.e. looks like, and the descriptions of what happened with the researcher should be in an addendum, not the description itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for Casimir effect :]

item.title: Re: Suggested remake
item.content:encoded: Yes! Do that, and I will change my vote. As of now, I see what the author is trying to do, but it's getting lost in the physics tech.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read it again after it was edited, and I still agree with Dexanote and Procyonlotor. WAY too much technobabble (even if it is accurate) before telling us what it is in a simple, easy to understand manner. Downvoted.

EDIT: Hell, I would advise just copy-and-pasting Voct's example early in the SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has probably been said a hundred thousand times, but way too much babble for way too little payoff. Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did my best to address the very valid criticism re: the Description lede, without (I hope) blowing the hook. It probably seems absurd that that's where'd I choose paucity of words, but there it is. If it works, props to Voct and Dexanote for the concise suggestions. If not - perhaps think of the article as a project to explore the limits of SCP-format science?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In reply to this and other people asking where the description of the object is: The description is there, it's just in the containment procedures and it doesn't specify that it's talking about the SCP. ECU-6852 is the SCP.

I've warmed up to this piece a bit more and changed my downvote to a neutral vote, although I still think a decent amount of the information should either be cut or presented in a less "in-your-face" way. I like how the format is not cliche and tries to surprise you, but I think more focus should be put into making the surprise itself (Instead of the build-up) more fleshed-out.

item.title: Re: Suggested remake
item.content:encoded:
Because honestly, right now it feels like the bastard child of the Handbook of Chemistry and Physics and The Aristocrats.

I found a Handbook of Chemistry and Physics at the clearance bin in a Barnes and Noble for only two dollars. The paper more than pays for that, let alone the contents. Plus, the expression on the cashier's face when we told him the price. He dropped his scanner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand this, I actually enjoy the way the technobabble reads and flows.

I cannot upvote without a clear, straightforward, basic physical description of the item/area to establish what it is. Even like, "SCP-XXXX is a complex establishment originally intended as specialized containment unit for item SCP-????. Containment procedures required [insert a plain and cliffsnotes explaination of the containment, and how 1718 originally met those needs]."

THAT is my issue with the article, and probably the issue everybody else has. It doesn't say what it is, only what it does and what it's done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a bit hard to understand, and I still don't fully understand the original purpose of the device. Maybe that's for the better, though! That said, I find the concept interesting. I think Voct had the right idea, maybe explain what it is in the description (though not exactly what it does) and it looks like it might be pretty solid.

item.title: Re: Suggested remake
item.content:encoded: This would make the article so much simpler to understand. My main problem with this is that, in-universe and out, when I read the description, I want to know very quickly what the thing is. At the moment, this article does not provide that information in a clear, sensible, or timely manner. With this change it becomes a lot more legible.
